aliases.name: Strix
aliases.meaning: owl-witch; ancient ancestor of the term

description.summary: The shtriga is a vampiric witch in Albanian folklore who feeds on the blood of sleeping infants. After feeding she can transform into a flying insect, and the only sure cure for her victims is a remedy obtained from the shtriga herself. Apotropaic protections include garlic, salt, the sign of the cross, and a cross of pig bone placed at a church door on Easter Sunday.
description.full: The shtriga (Albanian shtrigë) is a figure of Albanian folklore, particularly of the northern Albanian highlands and Kosovo, who appears externally as an old woman but at night feeds on the blood of sleeping infants. The name descends from Latin strix (the owl-witch of Roman tradition), and the shtriga is cognate with the Italian strega, Romanian strigă, Polish strzyga, and South Slavic štriga. After feeding, the shtriga is said to transform into a flying insect — typically a moth, fly, or bee. Children whose blood she has drunk waste away unless cured; only the shtriga who attacked the child can effect a complete cure, often by being persuaded or compelled to spit into the child's mouth. The folklore emphasises strong apotropaic measures: garlic worn or hung at thresholds, the sign of the cross, holy water, salt, and — in the most famous procedure recorded by Edith Durham (1909) — a cross made from the bone of a pig placed at the church door on Easter Sunday so that any shtriga inside could not exit. In Catholic northern Albania the shtriga was held in particularly clear distinction from ordinary witches, with Edith Durham, J. G. von Hahn, and later folklorists such as Robert Elsie providing the principal ethnographic record.

etymology.origin: Albanian shtrigë, descending from Latin strix ('screech-owl' / 'owl-witch')
etymology.meaning: Vampiric witch / nocturnal female feeder on infants
etymology.first_use: 19th-century Albanian-language printed records; Latin strix attested in Ovid and Pliny

related_phenomena.name: Strix
related_phenomena.description: Roman owl-witch from which strega, strigoi, shtriga and cognates descend

related_phenomena.name: Strigoi
related_phenomena.description: Romanian undead/vampiric figure cognate with shtriga

features.description: By day, the shtriga is described as an outwardly ordinary old woman of the village.
Distinctive pale or green eyes, often associated with the evil eye.
After feeding, the shtriga transforms into a flying insect — most often a moth, fly, or bee — to depart.

physical.morphology: Theriomorphic: human (old woman) by day; insectiform after nocturnal feeding.

abilities.description: Sucks blood from sleeping infants and small children, causing wasting illness.
Becomes a moth, fly, or bee to enter and leave houses.
Only the attacking shtriga can cure her victim — typically by spitting into the child's mouth or otherwise reversing the curse.
Direct gaze felt to be malicious; the shtriga is associated with the syz keq (evil eye).

patterns: Attacks infants in their cradles at night
Returns to a discreet daytime persona within the community
May be unmasked at Easter or at church

range.description: Northern Albanian highlands, Kosovo, and Albanian-speaking communities of North Macedonia and Montenegro; with a wider Balkan continuum of cognate figures.

first_recorded.source: J. G. von Hahn, Albanesische Studien (1854); later Edith Durham, High Albania (1909)

peak_activity_periods.description: Period of intensive ethnographic collection in the Albanian highlands.

origins.cultural_roots: Albanian highland folklore, in continuity with the broader Balkan and Romance witch-and-vampire tradition descending ultimately from Latin strix.
origins.earliest_accounts: Mid-19th-century records by J. G. von Hahn; early 20th-century field accounts by Edith Durham.
origins.folklore_connections: Cognate with strega (Italian), strigoi/strigă (Romanian), strzyga (Polish), and štriga (South Slavic); all descended from Latin strix.

timeline.event: J. G. von Hahn publishes Albanesische Studien, including notes on northern Albanian folklore
timeline.significance: Earliest major academic record of Albanian folk-belief in shtriga

timeline.event: Edith Durham publishes High Albania, recording the pig-bone-cross apotropaic procedure
timeline.significance: Most-cited English-language ethnographic source on the shtriga

timeline.event: Robert Elsie publishes 'A Dictionary of Albanian Religion, Mythology and Folk Culture'
timeline.significance: Modern reference compendium synthesising shtriga material

history.modern_era: The shtriga survives as a recognisable figure in Albanian-language popular culture and in Balkan-studies scholarship, and has been adopted into Anglophone popular media (notably the television series Supernatural).

documentary.name: Edith Durham, High Albania
documentary.description: Field-recorded accounts of shtriga belief and apotropaic procedures in the northern Albanian highlands.
documentary.analysis: Foundational English-language ethnography of Albanian shtriga belief.

paranormal.name: Real malign witch
paranormal.description: Within Albanian highland folklore, the shtriga is a real being — a woman with witch-power obtained through envy or childlessness — capable of supernatural harm.
proponents: Tradition-bearers in northern Albania and Kosovo (recorded by Durham, von Hahn, Elsie)
evidence_cited: Specific apotropaic procedures held to have demonstrably worked

skeptical.name: Folkloric explanation of infant illness and death
skeptical.description: The shtriga belief offers a culturally meaningful explanation for sudden infant illness, wasting, and death (including conditions later understood as SIDS, infectious disease, and malnutrition) and a mechanism (apotropaic ritual) for community response.
proponents: Folklore studies of European witch beliefs
evidence_cited: Convergence of shtriga reports with classes of infant illness
Cross-cultural pattern of vampiric child-killers across Europe

skeptical.name: Strix-derived cultural complex
skeptical.description: The shtriga is one of a family of European cognate figures (strega, strigoi, strzyga, štriga) descended from Roman strix folklore, transmitted across the Balkans and Mediterranean.

stories: Edith Durham's pig-bone-cross detection ritual (High Albania, 1909)
Recurrent village narratives of unmasked shtriga
folklore.beliefs: Held by tradition-bearers to be both real and historically operative; defended against by garlic, salt, the sign of the cross, holy water, and specific Easter rituals.

literature: Robert Elsie, A Dictionary of Albanian Religion, Mythology and Folk Culture (2001)
film_tv: Supernatural — 'Something Wicked' (Season 1, Episode 18, 2006), in which a shtriga is the antagonist
Lost Girl — episode featuring a shtriga (Showcase, Canada)

researchers.name: Edith Durham
researchers.contribution: Early-20th-century field ethnography of northern Albania, including the shtriga

researchers.name: Robert Elsie
researchers.contribution: Modern reference syntheses of Albanian folklore

organizations.name: Albanian Institute of Folk Culture (Instituti i Antropologjisë Kulturore dhe Studimit të Artit)

research.ongoing_studies: Continuing work in Albanian folklore studies includes documentation of regional variants and the comparative-Balkan study of the strix-descended witch complex.

sources.title: A Dictionary of Albanian Religion, Mythology and Folk Culture
